ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Malai Kofta exists; mapped to broader 'curry dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor. A more specific term for deep-fried dumpling curry is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 leaf node for Malai Kofta. Mapped to A0CKP (mixed cooked vegetable preparations) as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor; paneer and potato kofta in cream gravy falls within this broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Malai Kofta as a specific food entity; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 plate with 3–4 kofta balls and gravy
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Malai Kofta is approximately 400 g, comprising 3–4 deep-fried potato-paneer balls (koftas) submerged in a rich cashew-cream gravy, typically accompanied by naan or saffron rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR – National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single NIN entry exists for Malai Kofta as a composite dish. Values are adapted from NIN component data for paneer, potato, cashew, cream, and butter, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) entries for individual ingredients. The composite per-100 g profile reflects a weighted average of the standard recipe proportions described in the input record. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values were additionally cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cream and paneer.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson et al.) — analogous estimate based on potato-based mixed dish with high fat content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Malai Kofta. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy with potato-based mixed dishes containing high fat and protein, which are documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care). The substantial fat content from cashew paste, cream, and deep-frying significantly retards starch digestion, lowering the effective GI relative to plain boiled potato (GI ~78). A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (≤55) per the University of Sydney GI classification. gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 45.0) / 100 = 24.75, rounded to 24.8, based on 400 g serving containing 45 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (200 mg/100 g; ~800 mg per 400 g serving) combined with high saturated fat load from cream, butter, and paneer
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Malai Kofta to occasional small portions (≤150 g) and request reduced salt and cream preparation, as a standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 800 mg sodium and 25 g saturated fat, both of which elevate cardiovascular risk in hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-to-moderate glycemic index (55) combined with high energy density (162.5 kcal/100 g) and high saturated fat, which impairs insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat Malai Kofta as an occasional indulgence only; a standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 45 g carbohydrate and 650 kcal, and the high saturated fat content can worsen insulin resistance, making routine consumption incompatible with glycaemic management goals.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (6.25 g/100 g; ~25 g per serving) and cholesterol (20 mg/100 g; ~80 mg per serving) from deep-frying, cream, butter, and paneer
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of Malai Kofta; the high saturated fat and caloric density of a standard serving are inconsistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns, and the dish should be reserved for rare occasions with portion sizes limited to 100–150 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried components and high-fat dairy may promote pro-inflammatory pathways in susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor their response to Malai Kofta; deep-fried foods and high-fat dairy are potential dietary triggers for inflammation, though the cardamom and cinnamon in the dish provide minor anti-inflammatory phytochemical benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Malai Kofta is a rich, luxurious North Indian vegetarian main dish rooted in Mughlai culinary tradition, commonly served at festivals, weddings, and celebratory meals. The dish consists of two components: soft, deep-fried dumplings (koftas) made from a mixture of mashed potato and crumbled paneer (Indian cottage cheese), sometimes enriched with raisins or chopped cashews for textural contrast; and a velvety, mildly sweet cream-based gravy prepared by blending sautéed onions, tomato puree, and cashew nut paste, then finishing with fresh cream (malai) and butter. The gravy is seasoned with mild aromatic spices including cardamom and cinnamon, giving it a delicate, fragrant warmth rather than sharp heat. The texture of the koftas is soft and yielding inside with a lightly crisp exterior, while the gravy is smooth, thick, and coating. Nutritionally, Malai Kofta is energy-dense at approximately 162.5 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content (11.25 g/100 g, predominantly saturated at 6.25 g/100 g) derived from deep-frying oil, cream, butter, and cashews. Protein content is moderate at 5 g/100 g, contributed mainly by paneer. Carbohydrate content is 11.25 g/100 g, primarily from potato starch, with a low glycemic index of approximately 55 due to fat-mediated slowing of starch digestion. Sodium is notable at 200 mg/100 g. A standard restaurant serving of 400 g delivers approximately 650 kcal, 45 g carbohydrate, 45 g fat, and 800 mg sodium, making it a high-concern dish for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is best paired with naan or saffron rice and is not suitable for vegan, lactose-intolerant, or nut-allergic individuals.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Malai Kofta as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for paneer, potato, cashew, cream, and butter, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable potato-based high-fat mixed dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Malai Kofta as a composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024; individual ingredient fatty acid profiles exist but reliable composite proportions for this preparation are not published in an approved source. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation. 2. Portions: the input record contained four portion entries, two of which were exact duplicates (250 g, enriched_json). The canonical serving size used is 400 g (1 plate with 3–4 kofta balls and gravy) sourced from the input grabfood entry; the 250 g entry is retained as an alternative serving. 3. GI glycemic load calculation: GL = (55 × 45.0 g carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 24.75, reported as 24.8. 4. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values from the input were originally attributed to 'grabfood' (a forbidden source); these values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for cream and paneer, which support values in the same range, and are now treated as adapted estimates from approved sources. 5. This dish is not suitable for vegan, lactose-intolerant, or cashew-allergic individuals. 6. The dietary tag 'low_sugar' from the input cultural section is retained as contextually accurate (sugar 2.5 g/100 g) but does not imply suitability for diabetic management given the overall energy and fat density. 7. gi_category is set to 'low': per the University of Sydney GI classification, GI ≤55 is classified as low. The estimated GI of 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low category and is therefore correctly categorised as 'low', not 'medium' (which begins at GI 56).

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from input JSON, ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central sources applied, ontology anchors set with gap notes, health context populated with all three required conditions.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'. Verified gi_category consistency.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index of 55. Per University of Sydney GI classification, GI ≤55 is low; GI 56–69 is medium. Updated gi_source.source_match_notes and notes field to document this classification rationale. Updated llm_training.natural_language_description to reflect 'low glycemic index' consistently. Updated type2_diabetes key_factor to reflect 'low-to-moderate' GI language.
